retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Extracellular Vesicles -- Function -- The role of EVs in the Host-pathogen Relationship
retrieved_docs.content: Proteomic studies on bacterial EVs suggested that the protein cargo of these EVs have been loaded selectively from the cytoplasm, and not randomly, to perform these functions. [24] Also, bacterial EVs mediates the non-genetic acquisition of antibacterial resistance. One example is the ability of EVs to transfer b-lactamase protein to bacteria that are not genetically expressing it. [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Extracellular Vesicles -- Function -- The role of EVs in the Host-pathogen Relationship
retrieved_docs.content: On the other hand, EVs play essential roles in the recognition and destruction of the microbes by the immune system. Different components of the innate and the adaptive immune systems communicate EVs among each other to orchestrate their actions aiming at combating the invaders. This process involves EVs from several sources, including both the pathogen and the host immune and non-immune cells. Besides, the immune system utilizes these EVs as direct tools in their war against these attackers. For example, decoy exosomes carry molecules that neutralize bacterial toxins like diphtheria toxins and toxins released by Staphylococcus aureus. [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Extracellular Vesicles -- Function -- The role of EVs in the Host-pathogen Relationship
retrieved_docs.content: EVs isolated from different body fluids carry immune response-related and immune-modulatory molecules. These molecules include proteins, lipids, and nucleic acids. EVs released by different types of immune cells vary in their content and surface markers and perform different roles in the immune system. [31] A prominent example is that EVs from microbes, host dendritic cells, or B lymphocytes have been acknowledged as "miniature antigen-presenting cells surrogates" as they transfer antigens directly to the T-lymphocytes leading to their activation. [32]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Extracellular Vesicles -- Function -- The role of EVs in the Host-pathogen Relationship
retrieved_docs.content: EVs plays several roles in the war between the human body and its invaders. They are utilized by both sides, endeavoring to win the war. This process applies to all types of pathogens, including viruses, bacteria, fungi, and parasites. The pathogens, for instance, could use their EVs to evade the immune system through several mechanisms like complement inhibition and antibody degradation.  This process is in addition to the direct utilization of the EVs in their pathogenic actions like delivery of virulence factors, disruption of natural barriers, cytotoxicity, and modulation of host cells. [22] [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Extracellular Vesicles -- Function -- The role of EVs in the Host-pathogen Relationship
retrieved_docs.content: Another example is that MVs released from neutrophils under the effect of specific bacterial stimulation play direct antibacterial actions against the stimulating bacteria. [27] [28] Likewise, mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) and epithelial cells derived exosomes exert a direct antiviral activity through different mechanisms, including neutralization of the virus through the specific virus receptor loaded on their surface. Recent research illustrated such an effect against HIV by EVs derived from human placental trophoblast or CD4+ T-lymphocytes. [29] [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Extracellular Vesicles -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: EVs serve as vaccines in different ways. One example is the direct utilization of microbe-derived EVs as vaccines, which were proven to be effective in protecting against some bacterial diseases. EVs derived from the outer membrane of the gram-negative Neisseria meningitides, also called outer membrane vesicles (OMVs), are currently used as a vaccine against meningitis that is already licensed by several countries all over the world. Another way to use EVs as vaccines is to load microbial surface antigens on EV membranes. This method gives the vaccine higher potency than when using the soluble form of the antigen. Also, the EVs are efficient carriers for DNA and RNA vaccines. They protect the nucleic acid and increase the vaccine potency. Additionally, they are effective adjuvants for different types of vaccines. A unique advantage of the EVs is their potential to carry more than one component of the vaccine, giving a chance for higher potency. [39]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Extracellular Vesicles -- Introduction -- Source
retrieved_docs.content: EVs are secreted by all ranges of organisms, from prokaryotes to unicellular eukaryotes - like protozoa, yeasts, and fungi - to mammals. They can be isolated from all bodily fluids, including blood, plasma, serum, saliva, urine, sweat, cerebrospinal fluid, breast milk, and semen. In humans, these EVs have been reported to be secreted by nearly all types of cells in our physique, including but not limited to immune cells, endothelial cells, red blood cells, erythrocytes, liver cells, and epithelial cells.

retrieved_docs.title: Bacteroides Fragilis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Outer-membrane vesicles (OMVs) are vesicles that bud out of the outer membrane of bacteria and are commonly produced by gram-negative bacteria and may play a role in trafficking bacterial biochemicals. [11] Elhenawy et al. .demonstrated the preferential packing of acidic glycosidases and proteases into Bacteroide's outer membrane vesicles and proposed that OMVs contribute to the establishment and balance of the gut microbiota. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Extracellular Vesicles -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Another mechanism of EVs function is what we can call "the decoy" function in which surface proteins on the EVs membrane capture external molecules or pathogens to neutralize their effects. Whatever is the mechanism of action, the final role of EVs, either physiological or pathological, depends on their cell of origin and the conditions through which they have been stimulated. These factors determine the final cargo of the EVs and the lipid and protein content of their membranes, which, by turn, decides their ultimate destination and actions.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Extracellular Vesicles -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The engineered EVs also carry great potential as a therapy for several diseases; this includes cancer, immune-associated diseases, neurological, metabolic, and infectious diseases. Scientists adopted several strategies to engineer EVs for therapeutic purposes. One approach is to insert proteins on the membrane of the vesicle that has a therapeutic benefit or that target EVs to a specific type of cells like cancer cells. They also modified the content of the EVs to carry a particular protein or nucleic acid that would combat a particular disease. Grafting of the EVs with artificial nanoparticles is another strategy that could improve the kinetics or the targeting of the EVs. Many potential anticancer and antimicrobial therapies have been invented and at different stages of the trials. For example, EVs carrying ACE2, the receptor of the COVID19 virus, showed promising results regarding their use as antiviral medicine. [38]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Extracellular Vesicles -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Extracellular vesicles (EVs) are a cluster of nano-sized vesicles of different sizes, cargo, and surface markers that are secreted into the extracellular environment through a variety of mechanisms. They carry various components of the cytoplasm and cell membrane that are selectively loaded into these vesicles. They are secreted by all forms of living cells and play essential roles in different physiological functions and pathological processes. They also have been utilized as Diagnostic markers and therapeutic tools in several conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Extracellular Vesicles -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: In conclusion, the emerging field of extracellular vesicles allows for a greater understanding of the mechanisms of most biological processes and diseases. Besides, EVs are potential tools for diagnosis, prevention, and treatment of many diseases. The field is still in its beginning, and a lot of effort by physicians and researchers is needed to unlock these possibilities. Involving EVs in the curricula of undergraduate and medical students is necessary to push the process and get the utmost out of these potentials.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Extracellular Vesicles -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Salmonella -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The NTS typically causes gastroenteritis, while S. typhi causes systemic disease through the use of virulence factors. One type of virulence factor produced by S. typhi and not NTS includes the Vi antigen, which is a polysaccharide capsule that prevents phagocytosis and destruction from immune cells. Another virulence factor specific to typhoidal Salmonella includes the typhoid toxin. The typhoid toxin is made when the Salmonella bacterium is intracellular and secreted through vesicles to the extracellular space leading to reduced circulating neutrophils, lethargy, and other neurological complications. Other significant virulence factors include the flagella, which helps promote the bacterium’s motility and the type III secretion system, which allows the attachment and insertion process into human nonphagocytic cells. [5] Through the use of these virulence factors, Salmonella enters the intestinal epithelium through the M cells before reaching lymphoid cells of the Peyer patches. After crossing the intestinal epithelium, Salmonella enters macrophages and is disseminated throughout the reticuloendothelial system before causing systemic infection. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Extracellular Vesicles -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: EVs also play essential metabolic functions; this occurs by transferring enzymes and metabolites between cells or by performing extracellular metabolic activities. This reason is why some scientists call them "metabolic nanomachines." [20] Studies showed that EVs secreted by the liver cells contain hundreds of enzymes that belong to the different metabolic pathways. When these EVs were incubated with rat serum, they changed the metabolic profile of the serum, indicating a functional role of these EVs. However, the specific functional roles of these enzymes outside the cells are yet to be identified. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Extracellular Vesicles -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: EVs from the metastatic cancer cells stimulates the migration and invasion of less metastatic cells; this occurs through different mechanisms, including the transfer of proteins or mRNA that enhance the process of invasion and migration. Also, EVs from cancer cells carry molecules, such as matrix metalloproteinase, on their surface that assist in the destruction of extracellular matrices and so aid in the process of cancer cell migration. Another significant role of EVs is through the preparation of the metastatic niche to receive migrating cancer cells. [34]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1531
retrieved_docs.content: Viral antigens constitute the active ingredient of viral vaccines, providing the essential immunogenic component that stimulates the body’s immune response and confers protection against the targeted viral infections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2100
retrieved_docs.content: Injection or infusion of therapeutic or prophylactic substances and prophylactic vaccination or inoculation against certain bacterial diseases are both classified under the broader category of Therapeutic Interventions: Immunization, Injections, Perfusion. This parent category encompasses medical procedures that deliver therapeutic agents directly into the body, either through injection or infusion, as well as preventive measures such as bacterial vaccinations. By grouping these specific interventions within the same therapeutic framework, clinicians recognize that both the administration of therapeutic or prophylactic substances and the use of bacterial vaccines are integral components of immunization and perfusion strategies aimed at preventing disease and delivering treatment effectively.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1759
retrieved_docs.content: Prophylactic vaccination or inoculation against certain bacterial diseases is classified as a therapeutic intervention and is a subcategory of the broader group termed Therapeutic Interventions: Immunization, Injections, Perfusion. This relationship indicates that preventive vaccination against bacterial pathogens is considered part of the overall therapeutic strategies that include various forms of immunization, injectable treatments, and perfusion techniques, highlighting its role within comprehensive patient care aimed at preventing infection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 4 -- Community 2778
retrieved_docs.content: that can disrupt cellular membranes and provoke immune reactions, while human insulin is the principal hormone governing carbohydrate metabolism and energy storage. Toxoids, which are inactivated toxins, are used to safely stimulate protective immune physiology without causing disease. Together, these diverse organisms, molecules, anatomical structures, and pharmacologic agents illustrate the wide range of factors that influence human physiological processes and underscore their importance in clinical assessment, diagnosis, and therapeutic intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1547
retrieved_docs.content: Cannabis can be evaluated and qualified within the discipline of microbiology, indicating that microbiological methods are applicable to its study and classification. In the same field, bacterial vaccines are recognized as an allowed qualifier, meaning they are a permissible and relevant category of interventions within microbiology. Together, these relationships highlight how both Cannabis and bacterial vaccines are connected to microbiology, underscoring the importance of microbiological analysis in understanding the properties of Cannabis and in the development, classification, and use of bacterial vaccines.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 228
retrieved_docs.content: Finally, **Other puncture of artery** is placed under the parent category **Puncture of vessel**, reflecting its nature as a vascular access technique. The overarching class **Prophylactic vaccinations and inoculations** encompasses **Prophylactic vaccination or inoculation against certain bacterial diseases**, **human vaccination**, and the subset **التمنيع السلبي** (passive immunity), which denotes the transfer of pre‑formed antibodies for immediate, short‑term protection without active immune response generation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 4 -- Community 2811
retrieved_docs.content: Acetylmuramyl-alanyl-isoglutamin is classified as an immunostimulant, meaning it belongs to the group of agents that activate or enhance the body’s immune response. As an immunostimulant, acetylmuramyl-alanyl-isoglutamin can be used to boost immune function in various clinical contexts, supporting the body’s ability to recognize and combat pathogens or abnormal cells.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2351
retrieved_docs.content: Vibrio cholerae, the gram‑negative bacterium that causes cholera, is directly associated with cholera vaccines, which are formulated to induce immunity against this pathogen and thereby prevent the severe diarrheal disease it produces. Understanding the link between Vibrio cholerae and cholera vaccines is essential for developing effective immunization strategies, guiding public‑health interventions, and reducing the incidence of cholera outbreaks worldwide.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 779
retrieved_docs.content: network of peptide‑based hormones, enzymes, microbial agents, physiological qualifiers, deficiency states and drug‑related mechanisms that support comprehensive patient assessment, diagnosis and targeted therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1636
retrieved_docs.content: Lactobacillus is classified as a microbial agent‑containing product, meaning it is a microorganism incorporated into formulations that deliver live bacterial agents for therapeutic or health‑promoting purposes. As a probiotic bacterium, Lactobacillus is used in dietary supplements, fermented foods, and medical preparations to support gut microbiota balance, enhance immune function, and aid in the prevention or treatment of certain infections. Recognizing Lactobacillus as a microbial agent‑containing product highlights its role in patient care as a biologically active component of probiotic therapies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2568
retrieved_docs.content: Prophylactic vaccination or inoculation against certain bacterial diseases is identified as a therapeutic intervention that includes immunization, injections, and perfusion methods. This specific preventive measure is placed under the broader category of prophylactic vaccinations and inoculations, indicating that it has a parent relationship to that general class. The overarching class of prophylactic vaccinations and inoculations functions to classify and organize these preventive immunization strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 325
retrieved_docs.content: The DTPP vaccine is classified as a member of the “bacterial and viral vaccines, combined” group, indicating that it contains antigens from both bacterial and viral pathogens and is therefore a combined vaccine. In this ontology, the category “bacterial and viral vaccines, combined” is regarded as a specific type of vakcíny (vaccines), placing combined bacterial‑viral formulations within the broader vaccine family. Additionally, the classification system links vakcíny to a parent category of viral vaccines, suggesting that vaccines are organized under the larger umbrella of viral vaccine concepts while still encompassing formulations that also target bacterial agents. The term kombinované vakcíny (“combined vaccines”) is used to map the DTPP vaccine, reinforcing its identity as a vaccine that integrates multiple pathogen components. Together, these relationships illustrate how the DTPP vaccine fits into a hierarchical structure that connects combined bacterial‑viral vaccines to the general vaccine category and situates that category within the overarching group of viral vaccines.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 4 -- Community 2882
retrieved_docs.content: Bacterial vaccines are recognized as an appropriate qualifier within the discipline of microbiology, indicating that they are a relevant subcategory or descriptor in that field. Conversely, microbiology itself is accepted as a permissible qualifier for certain clinical conditions, specifically extra‑hepatic biliary obstruction and a range of other disorders affecting the stomach and duodenum. This bidirectional qualification underscores the role of microbiological concepts both in categorizing preventive interventions such as bacterial vaccines and in contextualizing gastrointestinal and biliary diseases within a microbiological framework.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1696
retrieved_docs.content: Deoxyribonucleases / Plasmin functions as the active ingredient of human fibrinolysin, a therapeutic agent that promotes fibrinolysis by breaking down fibrin clots. By incorporating the enzymatic activities of both deoxyribonucleases and plasmin, this formulation leverages plasmin’s natural ability to cleave fibrin and deoxyribonucleases’ capacity to degrade nucleic acids, enhancing the overall efficacy of fibrinolysin in clinical settings where clot dissolution is required.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 1 -- Community 158
retrieved_docs.content: vaccination or inoculation against bacterial diseases belongs to both the general group “Immunization, Injections, Perfusion” and the more specific hierarchy “prophylactic vaccinations and inoculations.” Abdominal hernia is identified as a subtype of the Arabic‑named condition فَتْقٌ أُرْبِيُّ, linking the English term to its traditional nomenclature. Human vaccination is positioned as a child of “prophylactic vaccinations and inoculations” and encompasses passive immunization (التمنيع السلبي) as a subordinate form, illustrating the connection between active vaccines and passive immunity strategies. Collectively, these interrelated entities and relationships create a multi‑layered classification system that unites pharmacologic agents, supplements, vaccinations, resuscitation methods, nutrition delivery, physical‑medicine modalities, cellular fractionation techniques, and both operative and non‑operative procedures, thereby supporting comprehensive documentation and coordinated

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 1 -- Community 103
retrieved_docs.content: interlinked qualifiers and hierarchical links provide a unified framework that maps diverse biochemical entities—hormones, peptides, enzymes, microbial products, toxins, and synthetic drugs—to their biosynthetic origins and physiological functions, supporting integrated understanding of metabolic regulation, drug action, and disease pathology. Clinically, deslanosid (a cardiac glycoside) may be administered for decreased cardiac output, exemplifying the therapeutic relevance of these qualifier relationships.
